Optimal average power for Polystyrene surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength providing good absorption in Polystyrene with minimal thermal penetration
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on Polystyrene surfaces
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management
Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without substrate damage
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of Polystyrene surface contaminants

Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning pattern

Typical range for polymer cleaning to avoid thermal damage; starting at 50W for light contamination on PS, max limited by PS melting point (~240°C) and low thermal conductivity. Source: Studies on Nd:YAG laser cleaning of plastics (e.g., J. Appl. Phys. 2018).

Effective for contamination removal without substrate ablation; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster for light; optimal balances fluence and coverage. Source: Industry manuals from IPG Photonics and laser cleaning process optimization papers.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of PS; shorter pulses (ps/fs) minimize HAZ but ns sufficient for contamination removal without deep polymer damage. Source: Laser ablation studies on polymers (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020).
Balances cleaning speed and heat accumulation in low-conductivity PS; higher rates increase throughput but risk melting. Source: Academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019).

Below PS ablation threshold (~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe cleaning; typical for removing contaminants while preserving substrate. Source: Fluence threshold measurements for polystyrene (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2017).
Smaller spots for precise surface prep on PS, larger for broad areas; 200 μm balances resolution and efficiency. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems.

Multi-pass for thorough removal of heavy contamination; single pass for light; recommended 2 for uniform PS cleaning without overexposure. Source: Case studies in industrial laser cleaning (Proc. SPIE 2021).
Ensures uniform coverage on irregular PS surfaces; 50% optimal for efficiency and minimal heat buildup. Source: Process optimization in laser scanning (Opt. Express 2016).
